question: Why does DeepReadonly<T> need to exclude Function from recursion?

options.text: Because Function is an object, and without exclusion TypeScript will try to make all internal Function properties (call, apply, bind, etc.) readonly
Because functions can't be made readonly in JavaScript
options.explanation: In JS a function is an object and its properties can be frozen. But in the types context, the problem is that recursion over Function properties distorts the function's type

options.text: Because TypeScript doesn't support readonly for functions
options.explanation: TypeScript supports readonly for function properties. The problem is that Function extends object, and a mapped type will change the function's signature

options.text: It's not necessary — DeepReadonly works correctly without excluding Function
options.explanation: Without excluding Function, the type will apply readonly to all Function.prototype properties, changing the type and making the function incorrect

question: What is tail-recursive optimization in TypeScript 4.5+?

options.text: TypeScript optimizes conditional types where the recursive call is in the last position, increasing allowed depth to 1000+
TypeScript automatically converts any recursion to tail recursion
options.explanation: TypeScript does NOT convert recursion automatically. The developer must write the type in tail-recursive form with an accumulator

options.text: It's a runtime optimization that speeds up TypeScript code execution
options.explanation: It's a compile-time optimization for type checking. It doesn't affect runtime — TypeScript types are erased during compilation

options.text: It completely removes the recursion limit for all types
options.explanation: The optimization only works for tail-recursive conditional types. Non-tail types are still limited to ~50 levels

options.text: S extends `${infer H}${D}${infer T}` ? [H, ...Split<T, D>] : S extends '' ? [] : [S]
S extends `${infer H}${D}${infer T}` ? [H, ...Split<T, D>] : [S]
options.explanation: This variant returns [''] for an empty string instead of []. An additional check S extends '' ? [] : [S] is needed

options.text: S extends `${infer H}${D}${infer T}` ? [...Split<H, D>, ...Split<T, D>] : [S]
options.explanation: Split<H, D> is redundant recursion. H is already the part before the delimiter and doesn't contain D. Double recursion is inefficient

options.text: S extends `${D}${infer T}` ? Split<T, D> : S extends `${infer H}${D}` ? [H] : [S]
options.explanation: This pattern doesn't correctly handle the delimiter in the middle of the string and loses parts of the string

question: Which statements about recursive types in TypeScript are true?

options.text: Recursive interfaces (TreeNode<T> with children: TreeNode<T>[]) have no depth limit — the limit only applies to conditional types
The accumulator pattern (additional generic parameter) enables tail recursion
Depth limiter via tuple [never, 0, 1, ...] decrements the counter at each recursion level
Recursive types are always slower than regular mapped types
options.explanation: Not always. Simple recursive types (DeepReadonly) are processed efficiently. Slowness depends on depth and complexity, not on the fact of recursion

options.text: TypeScript forbids recursive type aliases — only interface can be used
options.explanation: Since TypeScript 3.7+, recursive type aliases are fully supported. There was a limitation before, but now type JsonValue = string | JsonValue[] works

question: What's the difference between naive Reverse and tail-recursive Reverse?

options.text: Naive: [...Reverse<Rest>, H] — recursion inside spread; tail: Reverse<Rest, [H, ...Acc]> — recursion in last position
Naive uses infer, tail uses extends
options.explanation: Both use infer and extends. The difference is WHERE the recursive call is: inside a spread operation vs in the last position of the conditional type

options.text: Tail-recursive Reverse only works with numeric arrays
options.explanation: Tail-recursive Reverse works with arrays of any type. The optimization depends on the position of the recursive call, not the element type

options.text: There's no difference — both are limited to 50 levels of recursion
options.explanation: The tail-recursive variant is optimized by TypeScript 4.5+ and works with 1000+ elements, while the naive one fails at ~45
